title: Cervical cancer in Africa: the leading cancer killer of women and a clinical trial desert
primary_estimand: Condition Colonialism Index (CCI) for cervical cancer trials in Africa
certainty: high (registry data, complete enumeration within ClinicalTrials.gov)
body: How does Africa share of cervical cancer clinical trials compare with its overall disease burden globally? We queried ClinicalTrials.gov for interventional cervical cancer studies and classified each trial by location, intervention type, sponsor origin, and phase. Africa carries approximately twenty-five percent of the global cervical cancer burden yet hosts only about fifty registered trials compared to three thousand four hundred fifty-one in the United States, yielding a Condition Colonialism Index of seventeen point nine. Cervical cancer is the leading cause of cancer death among African women, killing an estimated eighty thousand annually, yet the three highest-incidence countries Mozambique, Malawi, and Zimbabwe each host fewer than five trials. Screening and vaccination studies outnumber treatment trials, but overall activity remains negligible relative to burden, and most are sponsored by non-African institutions with only a minority led by African investigators. The analysis is limited to a single registry and cannot capture studies registered only on national or WHO platforms.
ai_transparency: LLM assistance was used in early drafting. The final text was rewritten by the author, who takes full responsibility for the content.

checks.value: Africa: 25% burden, ~50 trials vs US 3,451 trials
Mozambique, Malawi, Zimbabwe: highest incidence, fewest trials
single registry, cannot capture national/WHO platform registrations
